en.extract:
The eastern whip-poor-will (Antrostomus vociferus; also called "whip-o-will", "whip o' will", etc.) is a medium-sized (22–27 cm or 8.7–10.6 in) bird within the nightjar family, Caprimulgidae, from North America. The whip-poor-will is commonly heard within its range, but less often seen because of its camouflage. It is named onomatopoeically after its song.


Description

This medium-sized nightjar measures 22–27 cm (8+1⁄2–10+1⁄2 in) in length, spans 45–50 cm (17+1⁄2–19+1⁄2 in) across the wings and weighs 42–69 g (1+1⁄2–2+7⁄16 oz). Further standard measurements are a wing chord of 14.7 to 16.9 cm (5+13⁄16 to 6+5⁄8 in), a tail of 10.5 to 12.8 cm (4+1⁄8 to 5+1⁄16 in), a bill of 1 to 1.4 cm (3⁄8 to 9⁄16 in) and a tarsus of 1.5 to 1.8 cm (9⁄16 to 11⁄16 in). Adults have mottled plumage: the upperparts are grey, black and brown; the lower parts are grey and black. They have a very short bill and a black throat. Males have a white patch below the throat and white tips on the outer tail feathers; in the female, these parts are light brown.
This bird is sometimes confused with the related chuck-will's-widow (Antrostomus carolinensis) which has a similar but lower-pitched and slower call.


Ecology
Eastern whip-poor-wills breed in deciduous or mixed woods across central and southeastern Canada and the eastern United States, and migrate to the southeastern United States and to eastern Mexico and Central America for the winter. These birds forage at night, catching insects in flight, and normally sleep during the day. Eastern whip-poor-wills nest on the ground, in shaded locations among dead leaves, and usually lay two eggs at a time. The bird will commonly remain on the nest unless almost stepped upon.
Eastern whip-poor-will are typically socially monogamous. However, a male whip-poor-will was once recorded attending the nests of two different females, possible because one female's previous mate had recently died.
The whip-poor-will has been split into two species. Eastern populations are now referred to as the eastern whip-poor-will. The disjunct population in southwestern United States and Mexico is now referred to as the Mexican whip-poor-will, Antrostomus arizonae. The two populations were split based on range, different vocalizations, different egg coloration, and DNA sequencing showing differentiation.


Diet
The diet of an Eastern whip-poor-will mostly consists of insects, especially moths, also beetles, mosquitoes, and many others.


Conservation

The eastern whip-poor-will is currently in decline, though they remain fairly common.  In 2017, the eastern whip-poor-will was uplisted from least concern to near threatened on the IUCN Red List on the basis of citizen science observations demonstrating a decline in populations of the eastern whip-poor-will by over 60% between 1970 and 2014.  Several reasons for the decline are proposed, such as loss of early successional forest habitat related to fire suppression and habitat destruction, predation by feral cats and dogs, and poisoning by insecticides, but the actual causes remain elusive.  Pesticides and intensified agriculture have led to declines in the flying insect populations that the eastern whip-poor-will depends on.  BirdLife International has stated that initiatives like the Conservation Reserve Program will be crucial in conserving the species and reversing its decline.


Cultural references
Due to its song, the eastern whip-poor-will is the topic of numerous legends.  A New England legend says the whip-poor-will can sense a soul departing, and can capture it as it flees. This is used as a plot device in H. P. Lovecraft's story The Dunwich Horror. Lovecraft based this idea on information of local legends given to him by Edith Miniter of North Wilbraham, Massachusetts, when he visited her in 1928. This is likely related to an earlier Native American and general American folk belief that the singing of the birds is a death omen. This is also referred by "Whip-poor-will", a short story by James Thurber, in which the constant nighttime singing of a whip-poor-will results in maddening insomnia of the protagonist, Mr. Kinstrey, who eventually loses his mind and kills everyone in his house, including himself. The bird also features in "The Runaway Slave at Pilgrim's Point", a poem by the English poet Elizabeth Barrett Browning, in which the outcast speaker asks: "Could the whip-poor-will or the cat of the glen/Look into my eyes and be bold?"
It is also frequently used as an auditory symbol of rural America, as in Washington Irving's story "The Legend of Sleepy Hollow", or as a plot device. For example, William Faulkner's short story, "Barn Burning", makes several mentions of whip-poor-wills: "and then he found that he had been asleep because he knew it was almost dawn, the night almost over. He could tell that from the whip-poor-wills. They were everywhere now among the dark trees below him, constant and inflectioned and ceaseless, so that, as the instant for giving over to the day birds drew nearer and nearer, there was no interval at all between them."
"The Mountain Whippoorwill" is a poem written by Stephen Vincent Benét about a fiddling contest, won by Hillbilly Jim, who refers to his fiddle as a whip-poor-will and identifies the bird with the lonely and poor but vibrant life of the mountain people. American poet Robert Frost described the sound of a whip-poor-will in the fourth stanza of his 1915 poem "Ghost House". This is notable in Frost's use of assonance in "The whippoorwill is coming to shout / And hush and cluck and flutter about".
Emily Dickinson wrote "Many a phrase has the English language - / I have heard but one - / Low as the laughter of the Cricket, / Loud, as the Thunder's Tongue - / Murmuring, like old Caspian Choirs, / When the Tide's a'lull - / Saying itself in new inflection - / Like a Whippowil -"
The chorus of George A. Whiting and Walter Donaldson's song "My Blue Heaven" (1927) begins, "When Whip-poor-wills call and ev'ning is nigh".
In the 1934 Frank Capra film It Happened One Night, before Clark Gable's character Peter Warne reveals his name to Ellie Andrews (Claudette Colbert), he famously says to her, "I am the whip-poor-will that cries in the night".
Hank Williams's 1949 song I'm So Lonesome I Could Cry refers to the whip-poor-will's sound in its opening line: "Hear that lonesome whippoorwill, he sounds too blue to fly". The chorus of Alan Jackson's 1992 single Midnight in Montgomery makes reference to this lyric: "Just hear that whippoorwill".
In the 1958 movie Thunder Road, Keely Smith sings "The Whippoorwill," a song written by Robert Mitchum and Don Raye.
Elton John and Bernie Taupin's 1975 song "Philadelphia Freedom" features a flute mimicking the call of the eastern whip-poor-will and includes the lyrics "I like living easy without family ties, till the whippoorwill of freedom zapped me right between the eyes."
The Pennsylvania-based Indie rock band Dr. Dog released their song "Lonesome" on their 2012 album Be the Void, featuring the passage "I had my fill of the Whippoorwill / When he broke into song I shot him".
The song, "Cockeyed Optimist", sung by Nellie Forbush in Rodger's and Hammerstein's South Pacific, mentions such bird, singing, "But every whip-poor-will / Is selling me a bill/ And telling me it just ain't so!"
In the novel Slapstick by Kurt Vonnegut, the narrator hears the call of a whip-poor-will, which the narrator referred to as a child as "The Nocturnal Goatsucker".
In the fifth episode of the Netflix animated series The Midnight Gospel, titled "Annihilation of Joy", the protagonist encounters a talking bird attached to a prisoner. The bird, voiced by Jason Louv, introduces itself as a "psychopomp or a whippoorwill" and explains the cycle of death and rebirth experienced by its charge, a prisoner caught in an "existential trap".
The whip-poor-will is also featured in the last line of the chorus of the song "Deeper Than the Holler", a song written by Paul Overstreet and Don Schlitz, and recorded by American country music singer Randy Travis, where the singer's love is stated to be "longer than the song of a whippoorwill".
The second verse of the song, "My Home Among the Hills", about the state of West Virginia contains the lyrics "Where the moonlit meadows ring with the call of whippoorwills".
The chorus of HARDY's song, "Favorite Country Song", contains the lyrics "Whippoorwills singin' 'bout summertime".


References


External links

Whip-poor-will Species Account - Cornell Lab of Ornithology

The '''eastern whip-poor-will''' ('''''Antrostomus vociferus'''''; also called "whip-o-will", "whip o' will", etc.) is a medium-sized ({{cvt|22–27|cm|in|disp=or}}) bird within the [[nightjar]] family, Caprimulgidae, from [[North America]]. The whip-poor-will is commonly heard within its range, but less often seen because of its [[camouflage]]. It is named [[Onomatopoeia|onomatopoeically]] after its song.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.floridamuseum.ufl.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/52/2017/04/hardy29sh.mp3 |title=Call recording}}</ref>

==Description==
[[File:EasternWhip-poor-will.jpg|thumb|left|[[Magee Marsh]] - Ohio (flash photo)]]
This medium-sized nightjar measures {{convert|22|-|27|cm|in|frac=2|abbr=on}} in length, spans {{convert|45|-|50|cm|in|frac=2|abbr=on}} across the wings and weighs {{convert|42|-|69|g|oz|frac=16|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/whip-poor-will/lifehistory|publisher=All About Birds|title=Whip-poor-will}}</ref> Further standard measurements are a [[Wing chord (biology)|wing chord]] of {{convert|14.7|to|16.9|cm|in|frac=16|abbr=on}}, a tail of {{convert|10.5|to|12.8|cm|in|frac=16|abbr=on}}, a [[Beak|bill]] of {{convert|1|to|1.4|cm|in|frac=16|abbr=on}} and a [[Tarsus (skeleton)|tarsus]] of {{convert|1.5|to|1.8|cm|in|frac=16|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{cite book|last=Holyoak|first=D.T.|year=2001|title=Nightjars and their Allies: the Caprimulgiformes|publisher=Oxford University Press|location=Oxford, New York|isbn=978-0-19-854987-1}}</ref> Adults have mottled plumage: the upperparts are grey, black and brown; the lower parts are grey and black. They have a very short bill and a black throat. Males have a white patch below the throat and white tips on the outer tail feathers; in the female, these parts are light brown.

This bird is sometimes confused<ref>For example, Henninger (1906) combines the old scientific name of ''C. carolinensis'' with the common name "whip-poor-will". As ''C. carolinensis'' does not occur in the area discussed, he obviously refers to ''C. vociferus''. In other cases, the specific identity of birds may not be determinable.</ref> with the related [[chuck-will's-widow]] (''Antrostomus carolinensis'') which has a similar but lower-pitched and slower call.

==Ecology==
Eastern whip-poor-wills breed in deciduous or mixed woods across central and southeastern [[Canada]] and the eastern [[United States]], and [[bird migration|migrate]] to the southeastern United States and to eastern Mexico and Central America for the winter. These birds forage at night, catching insects in flight, and normally sleep during the day. Eastern whip-poor-wills nest on the ground, in shaded locations among dead leaves, and usually lay two eggs at a time. The bird will commonly remain on the nest unless almost stepped upon.{{citation needed|date=December 2023}}

Eastern whip-poor-will are typically socially monogamous. However, a male whip-poor-will was once recorded attending the nests of two different females, possible because one female's previous mate had recently died.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Nighthawk |first=Asch M. |last2=Malone |first2=Kristen M. |last3=Straub |first3=Jacob N. |last4=Palumbo |first4=Matthew D. |date=2026-01-02 |title=A confirmed case of social polygyny in Eastern Whip-poor-will ( Antrostomus vociferus ) |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15594491.2025.2553423 |journal=The Wilson Journal of Ornithology |language=en |volume=138 |issue=1 |pages=157–164 |doi=10.1080/15594491.2025.2553423 |issn=1559-4491|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

The whip-poor-will has been split into two species. Eastern populations are now referred to as the eastern whip-poor-will. The disjunct population in southwestern United States and Mexico is now referred to as the [[Mexican whip-poor-will]], ''Antrostomus arizonae''. The two populations were split based on range, different vocalizations, different egg coloration, and DNA sequencing showing differentiation.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Chesser|first1=R. T.|first2=R. C.|last2=Banks|first3=F. K.|last3=Barker|first4=C.|last4=Cicero|first5=J. L.|last5=Dunn|first6=A. W.|last6=Kratter|first7=I. J.|last7=Lovette|first8=P. C.|last8=Rasmussen|first9=J. V.|last9=Remsen Jr|first10=J. D.|last10=Rising|first11=D. F.|last11=Stotz|first12=K.|last12=Winker|year=2010|title=Fifty-first supplement to the American Ornithologists' Union Check-list of North American Birds|journal=Auk|volume=127|issue=3|pages=726–744|doi=10.1525/auk.2010.127.3.726|s2cid=86363169|url=https://zenodo.org/record/1236285|doi-access=free}}</ref>

== Diet ==
The diet of an Eastern whip-poor-will mostly consists of [[insects]], especially [[Moth|moths]], also [[Beetle|beetles]], [[Mosquito|mosquitoes]], and many others.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Eastern Whip-poor-will {{!}} Audubon Field Guide |url=https://www.audubon.org/field-guide/bird/eastern-whip-poor-will |access-date=2024-04-20 |website=Audubon |language=en}}</ref>

== Conservation ==
[[File:Eastern Whip-poor-will PA.jpg|thumb|left|A rarely seen eastern whip-poor-will by day in [[Philadelphia]], [[Pennsylvania]]]]
The eastern whip-poor-will is currently in decline, though they remain fairly common.<ref name=lifehistory>{{Cite web|url=https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Eastern_Whip-poor-will/lifehistory|title=Eastern Whip-poor-will Life History, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology|website=www.allaboutbirds.org|language=en|access-date=2019-03-06}}</ref>  In 2017, the eastern whip-poor-will was uplisted from [[Least-concern species|least concern]] to [[Near-threatened species|near threatened]] on the [[IUCN Red List]] on the basis of [[citizen science]] observations demonstrating a decline in populations of the eastern whip-poor-will by over 60% between 1970 and 2014.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.seacoastonline.com/article/20110613/Sports/106130343|title=Sadly, call of whip-poor-will is being heard less|last=Hooper|first=Wayne|website=seacoastonline.com|language=en|access-date=2019-03-06}}</ref>  Several reasons for the decline are proposed, such as loss of early successional forest habitat related to fire suppression and [[habitat destruction]],<ref name=lifehistory/> [[predation]] by [[feral]] [[cat]]s and [[dog]]s, and poisoning by [[insecticide]]s, but the actual causes remain elusive.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.massaudubon.org/whippoorwill/index.php?type=declining|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070822221919/http://www.massaudubon.org/whippoorwill/index.php?type=declining|archive-date=22 August 2007|website=Whip-poor-will Project 2007|publisher=Mass Audubon|title=Massachusetts Whip-poor-will Project}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.wpi.edu/news/tracking-mysterious-whip-poor-will|title=Tracking the Mysterious Whip-Poor-Will|website=WPI|date=25 June 2018 |language=en|access-date=2019-03-06}}</ref>  [[Pesticide]]s and intensified agriculture have led to declines in the flying insect populations that the eastern whip-poor-will depends on.  BirdLife International has stated that initiatives like the [[Conservation Reserve Program]] will be crucial in conserving the species and reversing its decline.<ref>{{Cite press release|url=http://www.birdlife.org/sites/default/files/red_list_update_for_birds_-_two_iconic_species_saved_from_extinction_by_conservation_action.pdf|date=22 November 2018|title=Red List update for birds: two iconic species saved from extinction by conservation action|location=Cambridge, United Kingdom|publisher=BirdLife International|language=en|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200219215721/http://www.birdlife.org/sites/default/files/red_list_update_for_birds_-_two_iconic_species_saved_from_extinction_by_conservation_action.pdf|archive-date=19 February 2020}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.allaboutbirds.org/are-whip-poor-will-populations-declining-what-can-we-do-about-it/|title=Are Whip-poor-will populations declining? What can we do about it?|date=2009-04-01|website=All About Birds|language=en-US|access-date=2019-03-06}}</ref>

==Cultural references==

Due to its song, the eastern whip-poor-will is the topic of numerous legends.  A [[New England]] legend says the whip-poor-will can sense a soul departing, and can capture it as it flees. This is used as a plot device in [[H. P. Lovecraft]]'s story ''[[The Dunwich Horror]]''. Lovecraft based this idea on information of local legends given to him by Edith Miniter of [[Wilbraham, Massachusetts|North Wilbraham, Massachusetts]], when he visited her in 1928. This is likely related to an earlier Native American and general American folk belief that the singing of the birds is a death omen.<ref>Encyclopedia of Superstitions, p. 716.</ref> This is also referred by "Whip-poor-will", a short story by [[James Thurber]], in which the constant nighttime singing of a whip-poor-will results in maddening insomnia of the protagonist, Mr. Kinstrey, who eventually loses his mind and kills everyone in his house, including himself. The bird also features in "The Runaway Slave at Pilgrim's Point", a poem by the English poet [[Elizabeth Barrett Browning]], in which the outcast speaker asks: "Could the whip-poor-will or the cat of the glen/Look into my eyes and be bold?"<ref>{{cite web|url=http://classiclit.about.com/library/bl-etexts/ebbrowning/bl-ebbrown-runaway-1.htm|title=The Runaway Slave at Pilgrim's Point. Lines 55-56|author=Elizabeth Barrett Browning|access-date=23 October 2010|archive-date=16 March 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120316100633/http://classiclit.about.com/library/bl-etexts/ebbrowning/bl-ebbrown-runaway-1.htm}}</ref>

It is also frequently used as an auditory symbol of rural America, as in [[Washington Irving]]'s story "[[The Legend of Sleepy Hollow]]", or as a plot device. For example, [[William Faulkner]]'s short story, "Barn Burning", makes several mentions of whip-poor-wills: "and then he found that he had been asleep because he knew it was almost dawn, the night almost over. He could tell that from the whip-poor-wills. They were everywhere now among the dark trees below him, constant and inflectioned and ceaseless, so that, as the instant for giving over to the day birds drew nearer and nearer, there was no interval at all between them."<ref>{{cite web |title=Barn Burning |author=Faulkner, William |publisher=rajuabju.com |url=http://www.rajuabju.com/literature/barnburning.htm |access-date=1 May 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110521203954/http://rajuabju.com/literature/barnburning.htm |archive-date=21 May 2011 }}</ref>

"The Mountain Whippoorwill" is a poem written by [[Stephen Vincent Benét]] about a fiddling contest, won by Hillbilly Jim, who refers to his fiddle as a whip-poor-will and identifies the bird with the lonely and poor but vibrant life of the [[Appalachian people|mountain people]]. American poet [[Robert Frost]] described the sound of a whip-poor-will in the fourth stanza of his 1915 poem "Ghost House". This is notable in Frost's use of assonance in "The whippoorwill is coming to shout / And hush and cluck and flutter about".<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.bartleby.com/117/2.html |title=A Boy's Will. 2. Ghost House| author=Robert Frost|date=12 October 2022 }}</ref>

[[Emily Dickinson]] wrote "Many a phrase has the English language - / I have heard but one - / Low as the laughter of the Cricket, / Loud, as the Thunder's Tongue - / Murmuring, like old Caspian Choirs, / When the Tide's a'lull - / Saying itself in new inflection - / Like a Whippowil -"<ref>Dickinson, Emily. ''Many a phrase has the English language -''. Ca. 1862, winter. ''Dickinson's Birds'', dickinsonsbirds.org/project/poems/361.</ref>

The chorus of [[George A. Whiting]] and [[Walter Donaldson (songwriter)|Walter Donaldson]]'s song "[[My Blue Heaven (song)|My Blue Heaven]]" (1927) begins, "When Whip-poor-wills call and ev'ning is nigh".<ref>[https://yorkspace.library.yorku.ca/xmlui/bitstream/handle/10315/26703/JAC005105.pdf?sequence=3&isAllowed=y Sheet music published by Leo Feist Inc.]</ref>

In the 1934 [[Frank Capra]] film ''[[It Happened One Night]]'', before [[Clark Gable]]'s character Peter Warne reveals his name to Ellie Andrews ([[Claudette Colbert]]), he famously says to her, "I am the whip-poor-will that cries in the night".<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.imdb.com/title/tt0025316/quotes|title=Quotes from "It Happened One Night"|via=www.imdb.com}}</ref>

[[Hank Williams]]'s 1949 song ''[[I'm So Lonesome I Could Cry]]'' refers to the whip-poor-will's sound in its opening line: "Hear that lonesome whippoorwill, he sounds too blue to fly".<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.songfacts.com/lyrics/hank-williams/im-so-lonesome-i-could-cry|title="I'm So Lonesome I Could Cry"|publisher= Song Facts|access-date= September 25, 2020}}</ref> The chorus of [[Alan Jackson]]'s 1992 single [[Midnight in Montgomery]] makes reference to this lyric: "Just hear that whippoorwill".

In the 1958 movie ''[[Thunder Road (1958 film)|Thunder Road]]'', [[Keely Smith]] sings "The Whippoorwill," a song written by [[Robert Mitchum]] and [[Don Raye]].<ref name=varrev>{{cite magazine|magazine=[[Variety (magazine)|Variety]]|date=April 23, 1958|author=Whit.|page=7|title=Film Reviews: Thunder Road|url=https://archive.org/details/variety210-1958-04/page/n268/mode/1up?view=theater|access-date=May 12, 2023}}</ref>

[[Elton John]] and [[Bernie Taupin]]'s 1975 song "[[Philadelphia Freedom (song)|Philadelphia Freedom]]" features a flute mimicking the call of the eastern whip-poor-will and includes the lyrics "I like living easy without family ties, till the whippoorwill of freedom zapped me right between the eyes."<ref>"American Certifications - Philadelphia Freedom" Recording Industry of America</ref>

The Pennsylvania-based Indie rock band [[Dr. Dog]] released their song "Lonesome" on their 2012 album ''[[Be the Void]]'', featuring the passage "I had my fill of the Whippoorwill / When he broke into song I shot him".<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.lyrics.com/serp.php|title=Lyrics containing the term: whippoorwill|website=www.lyrics.com|language=en|access-date=2019-03-06}}</ref>

The song, "Cockeyed Optimist", sung by Nellie Forbush in Rodger's and Hammerstein's ''[[South Pacific (musical)|South Pacific]]'', mentions such bird, singing, "But every whip-poor-will / Is selling me a bill/ And telling me it just ain't so!"<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://genius.com/12259719|title=That we're done and we might as well be dead}}</ref>

In the novel ''[[Slapstick (novel)|Slapstick]]'' by [[Kurt Vonnegut]], the narrator hears the call of a whip-poor-will, which the narrator referred to as a child as "''The Nocturnal Goatsucker''".<ref>{{Cite book|title=Slapstick, or Lonesome No More!|last=Vonnegut|first=Kurt|publisher=Dell Publishing Co., Inc.|year=1976|isbn=0-440-58009-9|location=New York|pages=105–106}}</ref>

In the fifth episode of the Netflix animated series ''[[The Midnight Gospel]]'', titled "[[The Midnight Gospel#ep5|Annihilation of Joy]]", the protagonist encounters a talking bird attached to a prisoner. The bird, voiced by [[Jason Louv]],<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.duncantrussell.com/midnightgospel-jasonlouv|title=The Midnight Gospel - Jason Louv}}</ref> introduces itself as a "[[psychopomp]] or a whippoorwill" and explains the cycle of death and rebirth experienced by its charge, a prisoner caught in an "existential trap".<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.imdb.com/title/tt12176312/plotsummary?ref_=tt_stry_pl|title = "The Midnight Gospel" Annihilation of Joy (TV Episode 2020) - IMDb|website = [[IMDb]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://subslikescript.com/series/The_Midnight_Gospel-11639414/season-1/episode-5-Annihilation_of_Joy|title = The Midnight Gospel: Season 1, Episode 5 script | Subs like Script}}</ref>

The whip-poor-will is also featured in the last line of the chorus of the song "[[Deeper Than the Holler]]", a song written by [[Paul Overstreet]] and [[Don Schlitz]], and recorded by American country music singer [[Randy Travis]], where the singer's love is stated to be "longer than the song of a whippoorwill".

The second verse of the song, "My Home Among the Hills", about the state of [[West Virginia]] contains the lyrics "Where the moonlit meadows ring with the call of whippoorwills".<ref>{{cite web |title=Home Among the Hills |url=https://genius.com/E-w-james-jr-my-home-among-the-hills-lyrics |website=Genius |access-date=24 August 2023}}</ref>

The chorus of HARDY's song, "Favorite Country Song", contains the lyrics "Whippoorwills singin' 'bout summertime".<ref>{{Citation |title=HARDY – Favorite Country Song |url=https://genius.com/Hardy-favorite-country-song-lyrics |access-date=2025-04-14}}</ref>

zh.extract: 三聲夜鷹（學名：Antrostomus vociferus），是一種分布於北美的夜鷹。棲息於開闊的林地。波多黎各夜鷹亦稱為波多黎各三聲夜鷹。
